title: Find getNextLanes
prompt: Find the function in React's reconciler that decides which lanes to render next when the scheduler picks up work. Report file path, exact function name, a 3-8 line snippet from the function body showing the lane selection logic, and one sentence on what makes a lane eligible.

key_facts: function defined around line 249
selection flow: nonIdleUnblockedLanes = nonIdlePendingLanes & ~suspendedLanes
then getHighestPriorityLanes() to narrow to top-priority subset
falls back to pingedLanes if unblocked is empty

title: Trace setState to fiber scheduling
prompt: When useState's setter (`setX(v)`) is called, what eventually triggers React to schedule a re-render? Find the function responsible for scheduling work on the fiber. Report file, function, 3-5 line snippet, and a 2-3 sentence trace from setState to scheduling.

key_facts: chain: dispatchSetState -> dispatchSetStateInternal -> enqueueConcurrentHookUpdate -> scheduleUpdateOnFiber -> ensureRootIsScheduled
dispatchSetState in ReactFiberHooks.js (~line 3598)
scheduleUpdateOnFiber in ReactFiberWorkLoop.js (~line 973)
ensureRootIsScheduled in ReactFiberRootScheduler.js

title: Find Snapshot-flag call sites
prompt: Find places in React's reconciler where a Fiber's `flags` field gets the `Snapshot` flag set (bitwise OR with the Snapshot constant). Report 2-3 distinct call sites with file + line + one-line context, plus a one-sentence explanation of what the Snapshot flag does.

primary_sites.context: updateClassInstance bail-out path: getSnapshotBeforeUpdate exists + props/state unchanged
updateClassInstance else branch (shouldUpdate=false with later check)

bonus_site.context: host root reverted to client render from hydration

ground_truth.purpose: Snapshot flag schedules getSnapshotBeforeUpdate lifecycle to run pre-commit, capturing DOM state before mutations are applied.

title: Compare processUpdateQueue and prepareFreshStack
prompt: Compare two reconciler functions: `processUpdateQueue` and `prepareFreshStack`. For each, find its file + function name and write a one-sentence description of what it does. Then write a one-sentence comparison of when each is called.

A.does: Iterates the pending update queue on a fiber, applies each update in priority lane order, computes new memoizedState.

B.does: Resets the work loop and creates a fresh work-in-progress fiber tree before a render attempt.

ground_truth.comparison: prepareFreshStack runs once per render pass (in performWorkOnRoot / error-retry paths); processUpdateQueue runs per-fiber during beginWork.

title: Find Regex::find_iter
prompt: Find the function `find_iter` that returns an iterator over non-overlapping matches in the rust-lang/regex crate. There are multiple `find_iter` implementations across modules; report the one that lives on a `Regex` struct (the top-level public API), with file path, function name, a 3-5 line signature snippet, and one sentence on what it returns.

key_facts: method on the Regex struct (the top-level public API)
returns Matches iterator over non-overlapping matches
there are also find_iter on regex-automata Regex types (regex-automata/src/dfa/regex.rs:436, regex-automata/src/hybrid/regex.rs:399), those are lower-level

title: Trace pattern -> compiled Regex
prompt: When `Regex::new(pattern)` is called in the rust-lang/regex crate, trace the path from the user-facing entry point down to producing the matcher. Report the file + function of the top-level entry point, plus a 2-3 sentence trace through the key compilation steps (parsing → HIR → NFA or DFA construction → wrapping in Regex).

key_facts: Regex::new calls RegexBuilder or Builder::new -> build
parsing produces Ast (regex-syntax::ast::parse::Parser)
Ast -> Hir (regex-syntax::hir::translate)
Hir -> meta::Regex via meta::Builder::build_one (regex/src/builders.rs feeds meta layer)
meta layer in regex-automata/src/meta/ chooses strategy (literal/NFA/DFA)

title: Find call sites that select MatchKind::All
prompt: Find call sites in the regex codebase where `MatchKind::All` is selected or matched against (the leftmost-first/all-matches mode). Report 2-3 distinct call sites with file + line + a one-line snippet, plus a one-sentence explanation of what `MatchKind::All` means semantically.

ground_truth.purpose: MatchKind::All means the engine reports every overlapping match (typically used for finding all matches in find_iter, distinct from leftmost-first which prefers earliest+longest).

title: Compare PikeVM and BoundedBacktracker
prompt: Compare two NFA-based matchers in the rust-lang/regex crate: `PikeVM` and `BoundedBacktracker`. For each, find its primary source file + the type/struct name, and write a one-sentence description of how it executes a search. Then write a one-sentence comparison of when each is preferred.

A.does: Simulates the Thompson NFA in lockstep, tracking all possible states at each input position; guarantees O(m*n) time but doesn't support backreferences.

B.does: Standard backtracking NFA search bounded by a per-input-byte visited-set so it can't explode exponentially; supports capture groups efficiently when the haystack is small.

ground_truth.comparison: PikeVM is preferred for larger inputs where its O(m*n) bound matters; BoundedBacktracker is faster on small haystacks (under ~256 bytes by default) due to simpler control flow.

title: Find Flask.url_for
prompt: Find the `url_for` function in Flask (the top-level helper used in templates and views to build URLs from endpoint names). Report file path, exact function name, a 3-5 line signature snippet, and one sentence on what it returns.

key_facts: module-level helper function (not a method)
also exposed as a method on Flask via app context
returns a URL string for a given endpoint

title: Trace @app.route registration
prompt: When `@app.route('/foo')` is used, trace how the URL pattern + handler get registered in Flask's URL map. Report file + function of the top-level entry point, plus a 2-3 sentence trace through the key steps (decorator → add_url_rule → URL map.Rule).

key_facts: route() is a decorator returning a wrapper
wraps view func and calls add_url_rule
add_url_rule constructs a werkzeug Rule and adds it to the app's url_map

title: Find before_request registration sites
prompt: Find 2-3 distinct call sites in Flask where a `before_request` handler is registered (the hook that runs before each request). Report file + line + enclosing-function + a one-line code snippet per site.

key_facts: before_request decorator stores the function in before_request_funcs dict
registered per-app and per-blueprint
fires in the request dispatch loop

title: Compare Flask app vs Blueprint
prompt: Compare the `Flask` app class and the `Blueprint` class. For each: file + class name + one-sentence description of what it represents. Then a one-sentence comparison of how Blueprints attach to Flask apps.

A.does: Top-level WSGI application that holds the URL map, view functions, configuration, and request lifecycle.

B.does: A modular component that defines URL rules + handlers in isolation, then gets registered onto a Flask app at runtime via app.register_blueprint().

ground_truth.comparison: Blueprints are registered onto a Flask app via app.register_blueprint(), which copies their accumulated route registrations and handlers into the app's url_map.

title: Find Context.JSON
prompt: Find the `JSON` method on Gin's `Context` (used as `c.JSON(200, obj)` to write a JSON response). Report file path, exact function/method name, a 3-5 line signature snippet, and one sentence on what it does.

key_facts: method on *gin.Context
serializes obj to JSON and writes to ResponseWriter
sets Content-Type to application/json

title: Trace Engine.Run server startup
prompt: When `engine.Run(addr)` is called in Gin, trace how the HTTP server starts. Report file + function of the entry point, plus a 2-3 sentence trace through the key steps (Engine.Run → http.ListenAndServe → ServeHTTP dispatch).

key_facts: Engine.Run calls http.ListenAndServe(addr, engine)
Engine implements http.Handler via ServeHTTP
ServeHTTP dispatches to handlers via Engine.handleHTTPRequest

title: Find Default() middleware configuration
prompt: Find call sites in Gin where the `Default()` function configures its built-in middleware (Logger + Recovery). Report 2-3 sites with file + line + a one-line snippet showing the middleware being attached.

key_facts: Default() creates a new Engine and Use()s Logger() and Recovery()
Logger is in logger.go
Recovery is in recovery.go

title: Compare Bind vs ShouldBind
prompt: Compare `Context.Bind` and `Context.ShouldBind` on Gin's Context. For each: file + method name + one-sentence description of behavior on validation failure. Then a one-sentence comparison of when to use each.

A.does: Binds request body to obj using a content-type-derived binder; on error, aborts the request with HTTP 400 automatically.

B.does: Same binding logic but returns the error to the caller without aborting the request — caller decides how to respond.

ground_truth.comparison: Use Bind when you want default 400-on-error behavior; use ShouldBind when you need custom error handling or want to return a different status code on validation failure.
